If I were you, I would book the low season.

If the budget allowed it, I would upgrade the rooms.
Hi Andrea. My group wants a beach resort in August, but the budget is really tight. What would you do?
If I were you, I would look at the low season instead. The same resort would cost much less.
But the group insists on August. Is there any alternative?
If you booked a resort slightly inland, you would get the beach nearby and a better rate. It is a good trade-off.
Interesting. And what if they want everything included?
If they chose a tailor-made package, I could add transfers and dinners. It would give them peace of mind.
That sounds worthwhile. Honestly, I am having second thoughts about the beach idea now.
If I were in your position, I would recommend the inland option. It is flexible, and your group would still be happy.
If I were you, I would book earlier.
I would recommend the tailor-made package.
There is a trade-off to consider.
If you had more time, you could see both cities.
Let me suggest a smart alternative.

Hi Andrea, it is Stefan again. I have a tricky one for you. My most important client wants a luxury trip to Italy for eight people, but he only has the budget for four-star hotels. He also wants a private guide in every city. Honestly, if I promised all of that at this price, I would lose money. But I do not want to say no. If you were in my shoes, what would you do? Maybe there is a smart alternative. Could you call me back with a recommendation? I trust your judgement. Thanks a lot, and talk soon.

Welcome to this short segment on the art of advice. When a client makes a request you cannot fully meet, never just say no. Instead, offer a smart alternative, and soften your advice with the second conditional. In English, we say, if I were you, I would choose the inland hotel, or if you had a little more time, you could visit both cities. Notice the pattern: if plus the past simple, and would or could plus the verb. This structure sounds polite and professional, because it presents your idea as a friendly suggestion, not an order. It also helps the client see the trade-offs clearly. So the next time you give advice, remember: if I were you, I would use the second conditional.
